drop.rationale: Fine-grained subtype of negligence with no e-Legislation attestation; covered by the headword 'negligence'.
Archaic equitable maxim with no HK statutory attestation and marginal contemporary centrality.
Procedural notion of formal witnessing; more properly an evidence/procedure concept than a substantive civil-law category.
Adverbial morphological variant of 'contractual'; no independent conceptual content.
Obscure Latin maxim on executor's goods with no attestation and negligible contemporary centrality.
Narrow variant of the de bonis maxim; zero attestation and redundant with the headword.
Specialised trustee-de-son-tort doctrine; redundant given 'trustee' and 'fiduciary' are retained.
Archaic equivalent of mortgage; duplicative of 'mortgage' and without HK statutory presence.
Phrasal collocation equivalent to the retained noun 'intestate'.
Archaic morphological variant of 'divorce', which is retained.
Adjective with overlapping meaning across contract and trust contexts; low attestation and conceptually diffuse.
Office-noun derived from 'executor', which is retained; minimal independent content.
Narrow accounting-of-estate sub-concept; no attestation and not of domain-wide importance.
Temporal variant of 'tenant'; not a distinct civil-law concept.
Mental-state subtype of negligence; zero attestation and covered by the headword.
Subtype of the retained headword 'misrepresentation' with no independent HK attestation.
Latin gift/transfer qualifier; the substantive concepts (succession, trust, gift) are already represented.
Narrow commercial-finance variant of lease; low attestation and not central to civil-law doctrine.
Morphological variant of the retained headword 'leasehold'.
Historical constitutional-prerogative instrument; more properly a public-law artefact than a civil IP concept.
Archaic variant paired with 'dead pledge'; obsolete and redundant with 'pledge'.
Courtroom formula, not a substantive civil-law concept; belongs to procedural style, not the domain.
Past-participle morphological variant of the retained headword 'mortgage'.
Narrow procedural subtype of probate; the headword 'probate' is retained.
Negative-prefix variant defined against the retained headword 'probate'.
Costs-taxation formula, more properly procedural than substantive civil law.
Colloquial US cohabitation-maintenance concept with no HK statutory basis.
Doctrinal subtype of consideration; covered by the retained 'in consideration of' and 'valuable consideration'.
Morphological nominalisation of the retained adjective 'patentable' pattern; subsumed under 'patent'.
Adjectival variant of the retained headword 'patent'.
Past-participle morphological variant of 'patent'.
Adverbial form unrelated in practice to the IP noun 'patent'; zero relevance to civil substantive law.
English-court institution, not a HK civil-law concept; zero attestation.
Past-participle morphological variant of the retained headword 'pledge'.
Relational agent-noun derived from 'pledge'; the headword adequately captures the concept.
Narrow descriptive phrase, not a canonical civil-law concept; no attestation.
Archaic synonym of fundamental/repudiatory breach; 'repudiatory breach of contract' is the retained canonical form.
Secondary-transaction variant of the retained headword 'mortgage'.
Secondary-transaction variant of the retained headword 'pledge'.
Adjectival morphological variant of the retained headword 'restitution'.
Narrow residential-tenancy situational term; 'tenant' is retained as the canonical form.
Professional-role compound; the core concepts 'trustee' and 'fiduciary' are already retained.
Narrow secondary-security variant of 'mortgage' with no HK attestation.
Agent-noun redundant with the retained 'guarantor'; sureties cluster consolidated to a single canonical form.
Institutional-noun redundant with the retained 'guarantor'; sureties cluster consolidated to a single canonical form.
Non-technical collocation, not a recognised civil-law doctrine.
Adjectival property-state term; subsumed under the landlord-tenant cluster retained via the headwords.
Adjectival antonym of 'intestate', which is retained.
Rare nominalisation of will-making; 'testator' and 'testamentary' cover the cluster.
Gendered variant of the retained 'testator' cluster; no independent conceptual content.
Archaic deed-draftsmanship term; zero attestation and no contemporary centrality.
Adjectival morphological variant of the retained headword 'tort'.
Phrasal collocation conveying nothing beyond 'tort' itself.
Fundamental-rights/criminal-law concept; domain mismatch with civil law.
Abolished form of action; zero attestation and not of contemporary doctrinal importance.
Abolished form of action; covered historically by the retained headword 'trespass'.
Abolished Latin form of action; zero attestation and superseded by 'trespass'.
Archaic Latin form of action (vi et armis); subsumed by the retained 'trespass'.
Office-noun derived from the retained headword 'trustee'.
Hyphenated orthographic variant of 'underlease'/'sub-lease'; the cluster is kept via 'sub-lease'.
Synonym of the retained 'sub-lease'; one canonical form suffices.
Adjectival variant of the retained noun phrase 'unleased land'.
Narrow land-status collocation subsumed by the retained property cluster.
Negative-prefix morphological variant of 'patent'; zero attestation.
Quiet-enjoyment phrasal collocation, not a standalone civil-law concept.
Relational compound; the surety/guarantor cluster is retained only via 'guarantor'.
Relational compound of the retained headword 'tenant'.
Relational compound of the retained headword 'trustee'.
Administrative registry artefact, not a civil-law doctrine; covered by the substantive headword 'copyright'.
Ordinary verb with no distinctive civil-law meaning; classifier noise.
Participle form of the same non-doctrinal lemma.
Nominalisation of the same non-doctrinal lemma; arguably better placed in economic/competition law.
Foreign patent-office institution; duplicative with the retained international IP anchor (WIPO).
Foreign treaty body; zero HK attestation and redundant with the retained WIPO anchor.
Foreign IP office; redundant with the retained WIPO anchor for international coverage.
Former PRC IP office name; duplicative with CNIPA/WIPO and of marginal HK relevance.
Historical WIPO sub-organ; redundant with the retained headword 'World Intellectual Property Organization'.
Foreign patent office; redundant with the WIPO international anchor.
Policy/economic term rather than a civil-law concept; classifier noise.
Specific HK statutory fund; narrow institutional artefact rather than a civil-law concept.
Criminal/correctional collocation; domain mismatch with civil law.
Orthographic variant of the retained 'sub-lease'.
HK housing-policy programme; narrow administrative artefact, not a civil-law doctrine.
Negative-prefix adjective defined against 'contractual'; no independent doctrinal content.
Adjectival property-law term of narrow conveyancing use; subsumed under the retained real-property cluster.
Past-participle morphological variant of the lease cluster; covered by the retained 'leasehold' and 'sub-lease'.

curation_principles: The reduction of the civil domain from one hundred and thirty-six to fifty core terms was governed by four principles, applied in decreasing order of priority. First, surface-form consolidation: where the glossary contained an agent noun, an office noun, a nominalisation, an adjectival form, a participle, and a negated form of the same underlying concept, only the canonical lexical anchor was retained, on the assumption that a sentence-embedding model trained on naturalistic text represents the conceptual core of such morphological families through a single headword without meaningful loss. Second, archaism and form-of-action pruning: Latin maxims and abolished forms of action (de bonis testatoris, trespass quare clausum fregit, trespass vi et armis, testatum clause) were removed because they lack attestation in the contemporary Hong Kong e-Legislation corpus and are preserved only as historical residue in modern civil doctrine. Third, domain-integrity filtering: concepts more properly belonging to criminal, procedural, administrative, or public law (torture, may it please the court, post-release, Letters Patent, Copyright Register) were removed from the civil set, even where they incidentally appeared in the DOJ Glossary under civil adjacency, to preserve the methodological cleanness required for per-domain RSA signal and axis-projection centroids. Fourth, sub-area balance: the fifty retained terms were selected to cover the classical architecture of civil law (contract, tort, property both real and personal, family, succession, trusts, and equity/restitutionary remedies) in proportions that prevent any single sub-area, in particular the morphologically prolific property and IP clusters, from dominating the domain centroid.
